title: Trade Union Act 2016
chapters: 22 sections + 1 Schedule. Amends TULRCA 1992. CRITICAL TEMPORAL NOTE: The Employment Rights Act 2025 (ERA 2025) repeals the VAST MAJORITY of the TUA 2016. The first tranche of repeals came into force on 18 FEBRUARY 2026. As at March 2026, this Act is in a state of ACTIVE TRANSITION — some provisions have been repealed, some remain in force pending further commencement orders. Eileen must track the temporal state of each provision and advise accordingly.

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — Conservative Government (second Cameron ministry), Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills Sajid Javid
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Main provisions commenced in phases: ballot thresholds (1 March 2017), notice periods (1 March 2017), political fund opt-in (1 March 2018), facility time reporting (1 April 2017), Certification Officer powers (1 April 2022). The Act was FIERCELY opposed by trade unions and the Labour Party throughout its parliamentary passage. Professor Alan Bogg (Oxford) described it as 'authoritarian'. The ILO Committee of Experts raised concerns about compatibility with Convention 87 (freedom of association).

significantAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 (ERA 2025) — REPEAL OF THE VAST MAJORITY
significantAmendments.effect: The ERA 2025 repeals the great majority of the TUA 2016. First tranche: 18 February 2026. Further tranches: April 2026, August 2026, October 2026, 2027. The effect is to REVERSE most of the restrictions the TUA 2016 placed on trade union activity, returning the framework closer to the pre-2016 position under TULRCA 1992.

precedingDocuments.title: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 (TULRCA) — the parent Act
precedingDocuments.significance: TULRCA 1992 is the foundational statute governing trade unions, collective bargaining, and industrial action in the UK. The TUA 2016 AMENDED TULRCA — it did not replace it. The ERA 2025 repeals the TUA 2016 amendments, restoring TULRCA closer to its pre-2016 form.

precedingDocuments.title: Strikes (Minimum Service Levels) Act 2023 (REPEALED)
precedingDocuments.significance: The SMSLA 2023 allowed the Secretary of State to set minimum service levels during strikes in specified sectors (health, transport, education, fire, border security, nuclear). It was REPEALED on Royal Assent of the ERA 2025 (December 2025) — the first provision of the ERA to take effect.

leadingCases.name: No leading case law on the TUA 2016 provisions — most are being repealed before significant litigation developed
leadingCases.principle: The TUA 2016 was in full force for approximately 9 years (2017-2026) but generated limited higher court case law. Academic research (Lyddon, 2021) found that unions mobilised successfully to meet the ballot thresholds — far fewer ballots failed than the Government predicted. The 40% support threshold in important public services had 'limited independent effect'. The ILO Committee of Experts repeatedly called on the UK Government to review the thresholds.
leadingCases.significance: The TUA 2016's legacy is political rather than judicial. It was a signature Conservative policy that restricted trade union power. The ERA 2025 repeal is a signature Labour policy that reverses those restrictions. Eileen must understand this political context to give time-relatable responses.

parts.title: Part 1 — What the TUA 2016 Originally Enacted (Historical Framework)

sections.title: The original provisions — ballot thresholds, notice periods, political funds, facility time, Certification Officer
sections.summary: The TUA 2016 introduced six categories of restriction on trade union activity: (1) ballot thresholds for industrial action, (2) enhanced notice and information requirements, (3) time limits on ballot mandates, (4) political fund opt-in for new members, (5) facility time publication and restrictions in the public sector, and (6) expanded Certification Officer investigatory powers. Each of these is documented below with its CURRENT STATUS as at March 2026 — whether it remains in force, has been repealed, or is scheduled for repeal.
sections.text:
PROVISION-BY-PROVISION STATUS TRACKER (as at 29 March 2026):

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(1) 50% TURNOUT THRESHOLD (s.2)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: At least 50% of those entitled to vote must participate in a ballot for the result to be valid.
Current status: STILL IN FORCE (March 2026)
Scheduled repeal: The ERA 2025 will repeal the 50% turnout threshold, but ONLY AFTER electronic and workplace balloting has been introduced and its impact assessed. Electronic balloting expected August 2026. The turnout threshold repeal is expected AFTER August 2026 — exact date not yet confirmed.
Eileen note: As at March 2026, unions STILL need 50% turnout for a valid ballot. This will change later in 2026 but has not changed yet.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(2) 40% SUPPORT THRESHOLD IN IMPORTANT PUBLIC SERVICES (s.3)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: In health, education (under 17), transport, border security, fire, and nuclear decommissioning — at least 40% of ALL those entitled to vote must vote IN FAVOUR (not just 40% of those who vote).
Current status: REPEALED — 18 February 2026 (ERA 2025 s.69)
Effect: Public and private sector ballots now follow the same rules — simple majority of votes cast, with the 50% turnout threshold still applying (until that too is repealed).

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(3) ENHANCED NOTICE AND INFORMATION REQUIREMENTS (ss.5-6)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: Unions had to provide detailed information in ballot notices and on voting papers, including a summary of the matters in dispute, the types of industrial action, and detailed breakdowns of affected workers by category and workplace.
Current status: SIMPLIFIED — 18 February 2026 (ERA 2025 ss.70-71)
Effect: Unions now only need to provide categories, workplaces, and total numbers. Voting papers only need to ask which TYPE of action (strike or action short of a strike). The detailed summary of the dispute and granular breakdowns are no longer required.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(4) 14-DAY NOTICE OF INDUSTRIAL ACTION (s.8)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: Unions must give employers 14 days' notice of industrial action (increased from 7 days). The employer can agree to 7 days' notice.
Current status: REDUCED — 18 February 2026 (ERA 2025 s.72)
Effect: Notice period reduced back to 7 days. The employer can no longer insist on 14 days.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(5) 6-MONTH BALLOT MANDATE LIMIT (s.9)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: A ballot mandate for industrial action expired after 6 months (extendable to 9 months by agreement). After expiry, a fresh ballot was required.
Current status: EXTENDED TO 12 MONTHS — 18 February 2026 (ERA 2025 s.73)
Effect: Ballot mandates now last 12 months. This significantly reduces the frequency of re-balloting required for protracted disputes.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(6) POLITICAL FUND — OPT-IN FOR NEW MEMBERS (s.11)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: New union members had to actively OPT IN to contributing to the political fund (changed from automatic contribution with opt-out).
Current status: REVERSED TO OPT-OUT — 18 February 2026 (ERA 2025 s.61)
Effect: New members are now automatically opted IN to contributing to the political fund, with the right to opt out. Existing members who previously opted out remain opted out. The 10-year ballot requirement on maintaining political funds has also been removed.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(7) FACILITY TIME PUBLICATION (ss.13-14)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: Public sector employers (and some private sector employers providing public services) had to publish annual data on facility time — the amount of paid time off for union representatives. Reserve powers for ministers to cap facility time.
Current status: REPEALED — 18 February 2026 (ERA 2025 s.66)
Effect: Public sector employers are no longer required to publish facility time data. The reserve powers to cap facility time have been removed.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(8) CHECK-OFF RESTRICTIONS (s.15)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: Public sector employers could only operate check-off (deducting union subscriptions from wages) if the worker could pay by other means AND the union covered the employer's costs.
Current status: REPEALED — 18 February 2026 (ERA 2025 s.63)
Effect: Check-off restrictions removed. Public sector employers can operate check-off without requiring the union to pay administrative costs.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(9) PICKET SUPERVISOR REQUIREMENT (s.10)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: Unions had to appoint a named picket supervisor, inform the police, and the supervisor had to be identifiable.
Current status: REPEALED — 18 February 2026 (ERA 2025)
Effect: No longer required to appoint a named picket supervisor or notify police of the supervisor's identity.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
(10) CERTIFICATION OFFICER POWERS (ss.16-21)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
Original: Expanded the Certification Officer's investigatory and enforcement powers, including the power to investigate without a complaint, impose financial penalties, and levy trade unions for the cost of the Certification Office.
Current status: LEVY REPEALED (ERA 2025). Enhanced powers remain under review.
Effect: The levy on trade unions to fund the Certification Office has been repealed. The expanded investigatory powers are subject to further review under the ERA 2025.
sections.keyPrinciple: The TUA 2016 is a DYING instrument. As at March 2026, the majority of its provisions have been repealed or reversed by the ERA 2025. The 50% turnout threshold is the last major surviving restriction, and it too is scheduled for repeal once electronic balloting is established (expected after August 2026). Eileen must give TIME-SPECIFIC answers: 'As at [date], the position is [X]. From [future date], it will change to [Y].' The TUA 2016 cannot be treated as static law — it is law in transition.
refs: TULRCA 1992 (parent Act — the TUA 2016 amends TULRCA)
ERA 2025 (repeals the vast majority of TUA 2016)
GOV.UK Guidance: Trade union law: transition to Employment Rights Act 2025
Acas: Employment Rights Act 2025 — trade union changes
ILO Committee of Experts — concerns about TUA 2016 compatibility with Convention 87

regulatoryGuidance.title: Trade union law: transition to Employment Rights Act 2025
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The Government's official transition guidance explaining which TUA 2016 provisions have been repealed, which remain in force, and how to manage cases that straddle the transition.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment Rights Act 2025 — what's changing
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Acas guidance on the ERA 2025 changes, including the trade union reforms and their implementation timeline.

commonErrors: Assuming the TUA 2016 is still fully in force — the MAJORITY of its provisions have been REPEALED by the ERA 2025 from 18 February 2026. Employers and unions relying on pre-February 2026 legal advice are operating on outdated information

Not knowing that the 40% support threshold in important public services has been REMOVED — since 18 February 2026, public and private sector ballots follow the same rules. An employer in health, education, or transport who believes the 40% threshold still applies is wrong

Assuming the 50% turnout threshold has also been removed — it has NOT (as at March 2026). The 50% turnout threshold remains in force and will be repealed only AFTER electronic balloting is introduced and assessed (expected after August 2026)

Not updating internal policies and procedures to reflect the February 2026 changes — policies that reference 14-day notice periods, picket supervisor requirements, facility time publication obligations, or political fund opt-in requirements are OUT OF DATE

Employers in the public sector continuing to publish facility time data — this obligation was repealed on 18 February 2026. However, employers should still have reasonable visibility of facility time for management purposes

Not preparing for the NEXT wave of changes — April 2026 (simplified recognition process), August 2026 (electronic balloting), October 2026 (trade union access rights, duty to inform workers of right to join a union). Employers should be preparing NOW

Treating the ERA 2025 trade union reforms as purely pro-union — the reforms rebalance the framework. They make it easier for unions to ballot and take action, but they also introduce new protections for workers (e.g., automatic unfair dismissal for taking protected industrial action at any time, not just 12 weeks) and new duties on employers (e.g., duty to inform workers of the right to join a union from October 2026)

CRITICAL — dismissing employees for taking protected industrial action is now AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR at any time. Before 18 February 2026, the automatic unfair dismissal protection lasted only 12 weeks. The ERA 2025 removed the 12-week limit entirely. An employer who dismisses a striking employee AT ANY POINT during protected industrial action faces an automatic unfair dismissal claim with uncapped compensation. This is the single most important change for employers to understand

Not recognising that the industrial relations landscape has FUNDAMENTALLY SHIFTED — the combination of: simplified balloting + 7-day notice + 12-month mandates + electronic balloting (August 2026) + removal of the 50% turnout threshold (after August 2026) + automatic unfair dismissal for strikers at any time means that lawful industrial action is now significantly easier to organise, sustain, and take. The employer's best strategy is genuine engagement and early resolution of disputes, not reliance on procedural barriers that no longer exist

versions.text: FULL FORCE: all TUA 2016 provisions in force. 50% turnout threshold, 40% support threshold in important public services, 14-day notice, 6-month mandate, political fund opt-in, facility time publication, picket supervisor, enhanced Certification Officer powers.
versions.amendedBy: TUA 2016 as originally enacted and fully commenced
versions.policyRationale: The Conservative Government's stated objective was to ensure that industrial action had 'clear, positive support from union members' and to reduce disruption from strikes. Critics argued the thresholds were designed to make lawful industrial action practically impossible.

versions.text: TRANSITION STATE: majority of TUA 2016 provisions REPEALED by ERA 2025. 40% support threshold REMOVED. Notice reduced to 7 days. Mandate extended to 12 months. Political fund reverted to opt-out. Facility time publication REPEALED. Check-off restrictions REPEALED. Picket supervisor REPEALED. The 50% TURNOUT threshold REMAINS pending electronic balloting introduction (August 2026+).
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 2025 (various sections, commenced 18 February 2026)
versions.policyRationale: The Labour Government's stated objective is to 'make work pay' by removing barriers to collective representation and industrial action. The repeal reverses the Conservative restrictions while introducing new protections for workers and new duties on employers.

parts.title: Part 2 — What's Coming Next: The ERA 2025 Timeline for Employers

sections.title: The employer's preparation timeline — what changes when and what to do about it
sections.summary: The ERA 2025 trade union reforms are being implemented in phases. Employers must understand not just what has changed but what is ABOUT TO change, so they can prepare policies, train managers, and brief boards. This section provides the implementation timeline and practical employer actions for each phase.
sections.text:
ERA 2025 IMPLEMENTATION TIMELINE — TRADE UNION PROVISIONS:

═══ ALREADY IN FORCE (18 February 2026) ═══
✅ 40% support threshold in important public services — REMOVED
✅ Notice period — reduced from 14 to 7 days
✅ Ballot mandate — extended from 6 to 12 months
✅ Political fund — reverted to opt-out for new members
✅ Facility time publication — REPEALED
✅ Check-off restrictions — REPEALED
✅ Picket supervisor requirement — REPEALED
✅ Ballot and action notice information — SIMPLIFIED
✅ Automatic unfair dismissal for taking protected industrial action — at ANY time (previously only first 12 weeks)
✅ Certification Officer levy — REPEALED
✅ Annual return requirements for industrial action data and political fund expenditure — REPEALED

═══ APRIL 2026 ═══
⏳ Simplified trade union RECOGNITION process
⏳ Recognition ballot threshold changed to simple majority (removing 40% requirement)

═══ AUGUST 2026 ═══
⏳ ELECTRONIC AND WORKPLACE BALLOTING introduced (Phase 1)
— Hybrid e-balloting (postal distribution, electronic or postal voting)
— Pure e-balloting for all statutory ballots EXCEPT recognition/derecognition
— This is voluntary initially, becoming mandatory in 2027

═══ AFTER AUGUST 2026 (date TBC) ═══
⏳ 50% TURNOUT THRESHOLD — to be REPEALED once electronic balloting impact is assessed
— This is the LAST major TUA 2016 restriction
— When repealed, a simple majority of however many members vote will be sufficient

═══ OCTOBER 2026 ═══
⏳ NEW DUTY on employers to INFORM WORKERS of their right to join a trade union
⏳ Strengthened trade union RIGHT OF ACCESS to workplaces
⏳ New rights and protections for trade union REPRESENTATIVES
⏳ Protection against DETRIMENT for taking industrial action

═══ 2027 ═══
⏳ Electronic balloting for recognition/derecognition ballots (Phase 2)
⏳ Pure e-balloting becomes mandatory for all statutory ballots
⏳ Extended BLACKLISTING protections
⏳ Collective redundancy consultation threshold reform

EMPLOYER ACTION PLAN:

(1) IMMEDIATE (March 2026):
— Update ALL internal policies on industrial action, trade union relations, and facility time to reflect the 18 February 2026 changes.
— Brief the board and senior management on the changed landscape.
— Ensure managers understand that dismissal for protected industrial action is now automatically unfair at ANY time.
— Remove references to the 40% threshold, 14-day notice, and picket supervisor from all documentation.

(2) BY APRIL 2026:
— Prepare for simplified recognition process — if a union approaches, the recognition procedure will be streamlined.
— Review the employer's approach to union recognition requests.

(3) BY OCTOBER 2026:
— Prepare for the NEW DUTY to inform workers of their right to join a union — this will require communication to all workers, potentially at onboarding and at regular intervals.
— Prepare for strengthened union access rights — unions will be able to request physical or digital access to the workplace.
— Train managers on engaging constructively with union representatives who have enhanced protections.

(4) ONGOING:
— Monitor the introduction of electronic balloting (August 2026) and the subsequent repeal of the 50% turnout threshold.
— Recognise that the industrial relations landscape is shifting fundamentally — the ERA 2025 makes industrial action easier to organise and take. Employers who respond with constructive engagement rather than adversarial resistance are more likely to maintain productive relationships.

PRACTICAL EMPLOYER RESPONSE TO A LIVE BALLOT OR STRIKE:

If a union notifies the employer of an intention to ballot for industrial action, the employer should:

(1) DO NOT PANIC — a ballot notification is NOT a strike. It is the first step in a structured process.
(2) CHECK THE NOTICE — does it comply with the current requirements? (Categories, workplaces, total numbers.) If it does not, seek legal advice — but do NOT rely on technicalities as a strategy.
(3) ENGAGE — contact the union and offer to negotiate. The best outcome for both sides is a negotiated settlement BEFORE the ballot. Once a ballot is called, positions harden.
(4) COMMUNICATE — inform senior management and the board. Prepare contingency plans for potential action.
(5) DO NOT THREATEN — threatening employees with dismissal for striking is counterproductive and may now be unlawful (automatic unfair dismissal for protected action at any time).
(6) DO NOT INDUCE — offering individual employees incentives to not strike (outside the collective bargaining process) may be unlawful under TULRCA.

If industrial action takes place:
(a) You CANNOT dismiss employees for taking protected industrial action — this is automatically unfair at any time.
(b) You CANNOT use agency workers to replace striking workers (Reg.7 of the Conduct of Employment Agencies and Employment Businesses Regulations 2003 — prohibition on supplying workers to cover strikes).
(c) You CAN reorganise work among non-striking employees (provided this does not breach their contracts).
(d) You CAN withhold pay for the period of the strike (proportionate deduction — no work, no pay).
(e) You SHOULD maintain communication with the union throughout — the objective is resolution, not escalation.
(f) You SHOULD document everything — the notice, the ballot result, the action taken, your response.

OCTOBER 2026 — DUTY TO INFORM WORKERS OF RIGHT TO JOIN A UNION:

From October 2026, employers will have a NEW STATUTORY DUTY to inform all workers of their right to join a trade union. What this will look like in practice:

(1) The employer must ACTIVELY COMMUNICATE the right — not merely have a policy buried in a handbook.
(2) The communication should be provided at ONBOARDING (for new starters) and at REGULAR INTERVALS (for existing workers).
(3) The communication must be NEUTRAL — it must inform, not discourage. Language that subtly discourages union membership (e.g., 'we have a direct relationship with our employees and don't need a union') may breach the duty.
(4) The format is likely to be prescribed in regulations — monitor for the secondary legislation.
(5) Failure to comply will be enforceable — the exact enforcement mechanism is awaited but is expected to involve tribunal complaints and/or enforcement through the Fair Work Agency.

Prepare NOW:
— Draft a neutral communication template.
— Review onboarding materials to include the right to join a union.
— Brief managers that the duty is GENUINE — it is not a box-ticking exercise.
— Consider how the communication integrates with existing employee engagement: this is not an anti-union measure but a transparency measure.
sections.keyPrinciple: The TUA 2016 is not being repealed in one go — it is being dismantled in phases over 2026-2027. Each phase brings new obligations and removes old restrictions. The employer who waits for the next change to arrive before preparing is always behind. Eileen must help employers ANTICIPATE and PREPARE, not just react.
refs: Acas: Employment Rights Act 2025 — trade union changes
GOV.UK: Trade union law: transition to Employment Rights Act 2025
TULRCA 1992 (parent Act — as restored by ERA 2025)
commonErrors: Waiting for each change to take effect before updating policies — the implementation timeline is published. Employers should be preparing for October 2026 changes NOW, not in September 2026

Not recognising the STRATEGIC implications of easier industrial action — the combination of simplified balloting, electronic voting, longer mandates, and reduced notice periods means that unions can organise and take action more quickly and with less procedural risk. Employers should invest in constructive industrial relations rather than relying on procedural barriers

Not preparing for the October 2026 duty to inform workers of the right to join a union — this is a NEW employer obligation that will require communication to ALL workers. Employers should be drafting the communication now

Dismissing employees for taking protected industrial action — this is now AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR at any time (not just the first 12 weeks). Employers who dismiss striking workers face uncapped unfair dismissal claims

Not understanding that the ERA 2025 also introduces new PROTECTIONS for employers — the reforms include provisions for dealing with unofficial action, and the structured recognition process provides a clear framework. The changes are not one-sided

parts.title: Part 3 — Employer Policy Re-Write Guide: What Clauses Must Change

sections.num: Cross-reference: all TUA 2016 provisions repealed/amended by ERA 2025
sections.title: 10 specific policy and contract clause re-writes every employer should action
sections.summary: The repeal of the TUA 2016 by the ERA 2025 means that employer policies, staff handbooks, industrial action procedures, and payroll processes drafted under the old framework are now NON-COMPLIANT or MISLEADING. This section identifies the 10 specific clause-level changes that every employer should action, with guidance on what to remove, what to update, and what to add.
sections.text:
EMPLOYER POLICY RE-WRITE CHECKLIST:

═══ IMMEDIATE (should already be done — 18 Feb 2026 changes) ═══

(1) INDUSTRIAL ACTION POLICY — Notice Period:
    FIND: Any reference to '14 days' notice' or 'two weeks' notice' of industrial action.
    CHANGE TO: '7 days' notice (or such shorter period as the employer agrees)'.
    WHY: ERA 2025 s.72 reduced the notice period from 14 to 7 days from 18 February 2026.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: The employer may wrongly believe they are entitled to 14 days' notice and fail to prepare for action that can now begin after just 7 days.

(2) INDUSTRIAL ACTION POLICY — Support Threshold (Important Public Services):
    FIND: Any reference to the '40% support threshold' or 'important public services' ballot requirements.
    REMOVE ENTIRELY.
    WHY: ERA 2025 s.69 repealed the 40% support threshold from 18 February 2026. All sectors now follow the same rules.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: Health, education, transport, fire, and border security employers may wrongly believe that union ballots must still meet the 40% threshold.

(3) INDUSTRIAL ACTION POLICY — Ballot Mandate Duration:
    FIND: Any reference to '6 months' or 'ballot expires after six months' (or '9 months by agreement').
    CHANGE TO: '12 months from the date of the ballot'.
    WHY: ERA 2025 s.73 extended mandate validity from 6 to 12 months from 18 February 2026.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: The employer may wrongly believe a union's ballot mandate has expired when it is still valid.

(4) STAFF HANDBOOK / STRIKE POLICY — Picket Supervisor:
    FIND: Any reference to the union being 'required to appoint a picket supervisor', notify police, or provide a letter of authorisation.
    REMOVE ENTIRELY.
    WHY: ERA 2025 repealed the picket supervisor requirement from 18 February 2026.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: The employer may wrongly challenge lawful picketing by demanding a named supervisor.

(5) STAFF HANDBOOK / STRIKE POLICY — ⚠️ CRITICAL — Dismissal After 12 Weeks:
    FIND: Any reference to the employer being able to 'dismiss employees who have been on strike for more than 12 weeks' or 'the protected period of 12 weeks'.
    REMOVE ENTIRELY and REPLACE WITH: 'Dismissal for taking protected industrial action is automatically unfair at any time during the action. There is no time limit on this protection.'
    WHY: ERA 2025 removed the 12-week limit from 18 February 2026. Dismissal for protected action is now automatically unfair AT ANY TIME.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: ⚠️ EXTREME RISK — an employer who dismisses strikers believing the 12-week limit still applies faces automatic unfair dismissal claims with UNCAPPED compensation.

(6) FACILITY TIME POLICY (Public Sector Only):
    FIND: Any reference to the obligation to 'publish facility time data' or 'report facility time to the Cabinet Office'.
    REMOVE.
    WHY: ERA 2025 s.66 repealed the facility time publication requirement from 18 February 2026.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: Low compliance risk, but maintaining a defunct obligation creates unnecessary administrative burden.

(7) PAYROLL / UNION SUBSCRIPTION — Check-Off (Public Sector Only):
    FIND: Any clause requiring the union to 'reimburse the employer for the cost of operating check-off' or requiring workers to have 'an alternative means of paying union subscriptions'.
    REMOVE.
    WHY: ERA 2025 s.63 repealed these restrictions from 18 February 2026.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: The employer may wrongly charge the union for check-off administration or refuse to operate check-off without alternative payment options.

═══ BY APRIL 2026 ═══

(8) UNION RECOGNITION PROCEDURE:
    FIND: Any reference to the '40% of eligible voters must vote in favour' threshold for recognition ballots.
    CHANGE TO: 'A simple majority of those voting is sufficient for recognition.'
    WHY: ERA 2025 simplifies the recognition process from April 2026, removing the 40% threshold.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: The employer may misunderstand the threshold and wrongly challenge a valid recognition ballot.

═══ BY OCTOBER 2026 ═══

(9) ONBOARDING / NEW STARTER MATERIALS:
    ADD: A clear, neutral communication informing all new workers of their statutory right to join a trade union.
    WHY: ERA 2025 introduces a new duty on employers to inform workers of their right to join a union from October 2026.
    WHAT TO SAY: 'You have a statutory right to join a trade union. A trade union can represent your interests in the workplace, including in relation to pay, working conditions, and workplace disputes. You are not required to join a union, and no detriment will arise from your decision either way.'
    WHAT NOT TO SAY: Do not discourage union membership. Do not suggest that the employer 'does not need' a union. The duty requires NEUTRAL information.
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: Failure to comply with the duty will be enforceable — mechanism awaited.

(10) DETRIMENT / WHISTLEBLOWING POLICY:
    ADD: Protection from detriment for taking protected industrial action.
    WHY: ERA 2025 introduces protection from DETRIMENT (in addition to existing unfair dismissal protection) from October 2026. Workers who suffer any detrimental treatment for participating in lawful industrial action can bring a tribunal complaint.
    WHAT TO ADD: 'No worker will be subjected to any detriment by any act, or deliberate failure to act, by the employer because the worker took, proposed to take, or was believed to have taken protected industrial action.'
    RISK IF NOT UPDATED: Employers may take action short of dismissal (e.g., withholding bonuses, passing over for promotion, changing duties) against strikers, believing this is permissible. From October 2026, it is not.
sections.keyPrinciple: Every employer should conduct a FULL POLICY AUDIT against this 10-point checklist. The most dangerous clause is number 5 — any reference to dismissing strikers after 12 weeks. An employer who acts on outdated policy language faces automatic unfair dismissal claims with uncapped compensation. The re-write is not optional — it is a risk mitigation necessity.
refs: GOV.UK: Trade union law: transition to Employment Rights Act 2025
ACAS Code of Practice on Industrial Action Ballots (updated)
ACAS Code of Practice on Picketing (updated)
commonErrors: Not conducting a policy audit at all — many employers have not reviewed their industrial action, staff handbook, and union-related policies since the ERA 2025 changes took effect on 18 February 2026. Every policy that references the TUA 2016 provisions is now out of date

Updating the policy document but not briefing managers — a compliant policy is worthless if the managers who implement it are operating on outdated knowledge. Every manager with responsibility for union relations or industrial action must be briefed on the changes

Believing that clause 5 (12-week dismissal) is a low risk because 'we would never dismiss strikers' — the risk is not limited to deliberate dismissal. It extends to any dismissal during or after industrial action where the action is a contributing factor. The policy must be clear

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 — 50% turnout threshold repeal (date TBC, after August 2026)
pendingAmendments.status: The last major TUA 2016 restriction. Will be repealed after electronic balloting is introduced and its impact assessed. The Government must review the impact before commencing the repeal. Monitor for commencement order.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — removes the final procedural barrier to industrial action

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 — employer duty to inform workers of right to join a union (October 2026)
pendingAmendments.status: New employer obligation. Regulations and guidance awaited. All employers will need to communicate the right to join a union to all workers.
Strengthened right of access for trade unions to workplaces. Physical and digital access. Regulations and guidance awaited.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — employers must facilitate union access

pendingAmendments.status: Electronic and workplace balloting will be introduced in phases. Phase 1 (August 2026): hybrid e-balloting for all statutory ballots, pure e-balloting for all except recognition/derecognition. Phase 2 (2027): pure e-balloting for recognition/derecognition. Voluntary initially, mandatory from 2027.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — precursor to the 50% turnout threshold repeal

surveillanceStatus.status: ACTIVE TRANSITION — multiple ERA 2025 commencement dates pending
